questions.question:
Let P be a singly linked list. Let Q be the pointer to an intermediate node x in the list. What is the worst-case time complexity of the best-known algorithm to delete the node x from the list?

A. O(n)
B. O(log^2 n)
C. O(log n)
D. O(1)
questions.to_find: Worst-case time complexity of the best-known algorithm to delete an intermediate node given a pointer to it.

understand.plain: We have a singly linked list and a direct pointer Q to some intermediate node x. Normally deletion requires finding the previous node (O(n)), but there is a clever trick: copy the next node's data into x and delete the next node instead. This achieves O(1) time.

keywords.explain: A list where each node has a value and a pointer to the next node only (no backward pointer).
To delete node x given only a pointer to it (no pointer to predecessor), copy the value from x->next into x, then unlink x->next. This effectively deletes the intended node in O(1).
keywords.example: Delete node 2 in 1->2->3->4: copy 3 into node 2, then set node2->next = node3->next, giving 1->3->4.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 560 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='50' width='80' height='40' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='50' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><rect x='130' y='50' width='80' height='40' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='170' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>x (Q)</text><rect x='250' y='50' width='80' height='40' class='ok' rx='6'/><text x='290' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink' style='fill:#fff'>B</text><rect x='370' y='50' width='80' height='40' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='410' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><line x1='90' y1='70' x2='130' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='210' y1='70' x2='250' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='330' y1='70' x2='370' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><text x='170' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Step 1: Copy B value into x</text><text x='170' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Step 2: Unlink node B -> O(1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Singly linked list A -> x(Q) -> B -> C. The trick: copy B's value into x, then set x->next = B->next, effectively deleting x in O(1).

given.aim: Find the minimum possible time complexity to delete an intermediate node when a direct pointer to it is given.

terms.connects: The structure within which deletion must occur

terms.meaning: A pointer to an intermediate node x that must be deleted
terms.example: Q points to node containing 2 in 1->2->3->4
terms.connects: Direct access to x enables the O(1) copy-and-skip trick

terms.meaning: A node that is neither the head nor the tail of the list (has both predecessor and successor)
terms.connects: Guarantees x->next exists, making the copy trick valid

given.plan: Instead of traversing from the head to find the predecessor of x (which would be O(n)), copy the data from x->next into x and update x->next to skip the original x->next node. This requires only constant time operations.

steps.title: Identify the naive approach and its cost
steps.formula_raw: Traverse from head to find predecessor of x: O(n)
steps.apply: In a singly linked list, to delete node x normally one must find the node prev such that prev->next == x, then set prev->next = x->next. Since we only have a forward pointer, a scan from the head is O(n).
steps.note: This O(n) approach is what most people first think of, but it is not optimal.

steps.title: Apply the O(1) copy-and-skip trick
steps.apply: Since x is an intermediate node, x->next exists. Copy the value from x->next into x (x->value = x->next->value), then set x->next = x->next->next. Free the old x->next node. This completes deletion in O(1) — no traversal needed.
steps.note: This trick works ONLY for intermediate nodes (not the last node) because we need x->next to exist. The problem guarantees x is intermediate, so this is valid.

solution.result: The best-known algorithm runs in O(1) worst-case time. Answer: D. O(1)

questions.formula_note: Copy-and-skip trick for singly linked list node deletion: copy successor's data into current node and unlink the successor, achieving O(1) deletion without needing the predecessor pointer.

questions.to_find: Contents of the singly linked list 1->2->3->4->5->6->7 after rearrange() completes.

understand.plain: The function walks the list two nodes at a time (p and q are adjacent pairs: p points to an odd-position node, q to the next even-position node). At each step it swaps the values of p and q, then advances both pointers by two nodes. The net effect is swapping each adjacent pair: (1,2), (3,4), (5,6), leaving the last node (7) untouched since 7 has no pair.

keywords.explain: Swapping the values of two consecutive nodes (p and q = p->next) in a linked list without changing pointer structure.
keywords.example: Nodes with values 1 and 2: after swap p->value=2, q->value=1.

keywords.explain: Using two pointers that always point to adjacent nodes and advance by two steps each iteration to process pairs.
keywords.example: Start: p=node1, q=node2; after one step: p=node3, q=node4.

x='10' y='190' class='sub mut'>Pairs (1,2),(3,4),(5,6) swapped; node 7 unchanged (no pair)</text></svg>

given.aim: Trace the rearrange() function on the list 1->2->3->4->5->6->7 and determine the final ordering.

terms.meaning: p = list (node 1), q = list->next (node 2); they are initialized as an adjacent pair at the start of each iteration.
terms.example: p->value=1, q->value=2 at start of first iteration.
terms.connects: These two pointers define the pair whose values are swapped each iteration.

terms.meaning: Standard three-variable integer swap: temp=p->value; p->value=q->value; q->value=temp.
terms.connects: Exchanges the values of the current pair in-place.

terms.meaning: After swapping, p moves to q->next and q moves to p->next (the pair two positions ahead).
terms.example: After swapping nodes 1&2, p jumps to node 3, q jumps to node 4.
terms.connects: Ensures each pair (1-2), (3-4), (5-6) is processed exactly once; if p is NULL, q=0 ends the loop.

given.plan: Simulate the loop iteration by iteration: each iteration swaps values at positions (1,2), then (3,4), then (5,6). Node 7 at position 7 is odd-positioned with no partner, so the loop ends without touching it.

steps.title: Iteration 1: swap nodes at positions 1 and 2
steps.apply: p = node1 (value=1), q = node2 (value=2). Swap: node1->value=2, node2->value=1. List: 2->1->3->4->5->6->7. Advance: p = q->next = node3; q = p->next = node4.
steps.note: After the swap the first pair becomes (2,1).

steps.title: Iteration 2: swap nodes at positions 3 and 4
steps.apply: p = node3 (value=3), q = node4 (value=4). Swap: node3->value=4, node4->value=3. List: 2->1->4->3->5->6->7. Advance: p = q->next = node5; q = p->next = node6.

steps.title: Iteration 3: swap nodes at positions 5 and 6
steps.apply: p = node5 (value=5), q = node6 (value=6). Swap: node5->value=6, node6->value=5. List: 2->1->4->3->6->5->7. Advance: p = q->next = node7; q = p->next = NULL (node7->next is NULL), so q = 0.

steps.title: Loop termination: node 7 has no pair
steps.apply: p = node7, p->next = NULL, so q = NULL. The while(q) condition fails. Loop exits. Node 7 retains its value. Final list: 2->1->4->3->6->5->7.
steps.note: The 7th node (odd-count tail) is never swapped because it has no partner.

solution.result: The list after execution is 2, 1, 4, 3, 6, 5, 7. Answer: B. 2, 1, 4, 3, 6, 5, 7

questions.formula_note: Adjacent-pair swap traversal: process a singly linked list in steps of two using (p, q=p->next) pairs; swap values at each pair; advance p=q->next, q=p?p->next:NULL.
